literal: an apparent transformation, a turning-into-the-appearance-of; from vi- + √vṛt 'to turn'
invariant_definition: Apparent transformation: a transformation in which the substrate appears to undergo change without really being modified (the rope-snake analogy: the rope appears as a snake without becoming one). Vivarta-vāda is the Advaita position on the *brahman*-world relation, opposed to *pariṇāma-vāda* (real transformation). All non-Advaita Vedāntic schools reject *vivarta-vāda*; Bhāskara (*pariṇāma-vāda*), Viśiṣṭādvaita (real *pariṇāma* of *acit*), Dvaita (real causation by Viṣṇu), and Vallabha (*avikṛta-pariṇāma* — transformation without modification, structurally a third option) all repudiate the Advaita doctrine.

per_school.definition: *Vivarta-vāda*: *brahman* appears as the world without itself being modified. The world is *vivarta* — *anirvacanīya* appearance — not a real *pariṇāma*. The rope-snake (*rajju-sarpa*) analogy is canonical.
*Vivarta-vāda* is rejected as a Buddhist smuggling (the famous *pracchanna-bauddha* charge against Śaṅkara). The world is real *pariṇāma* of *brahman*.
*Vivarta-vāda* in the Advaita sense is rejected; the world is real, produced by real causal action of Viṣṇu (D) or by *acit*'s real *pariṇāma* under the Lord (V).

*Avikṛta-pariṇāma* — transformation without modification: a structurally distinctive third position. *Brahman* really transforms into the world without losing or modifying its essence. Both *vivarta* (no real transformation) and ordinary *pariṇāma* (transformation involving modification) are rejected.

translator_note: 'Apparent transformation' is acceptable. The *vivarta*-*pariṇāma* opposition is the core metaphysical disagreement of post-Śaṅkara Vedānta and should be flagged as such for English readers.
